### Haley — 2015-01-09T01:47:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leaky-gut-protocol-optimizing-ketosis.12560/post-152172>

Inger-thank you for your response!

The turkey and chicken I buy are antibiotic/hormone free but yes probably grain fed as I don’t think they sell “bug fed” chickens here lol. I do try and consume more red meat than bird meat. I have tried organ meats but am always skeptical about the sources. However I do have access to some local grassfed liver. What about bone broth? Haven’t made any in a while!

On my walk in the snow this morning I was pondering the likelihood that I would not be consuming veggies this time of year 1000s of years ago. (one exception i did think of was winter squash as it does store very well-any thoughts?). But getting rid of my veggies would be extremely difficult for me as I rely on their fiber and nutrients! Maybe I just need more time and scientific evidence before I can take that leap. I also try and avoid raw as my digestion is rather crappy. When you say bitters…could you give me some examples and where I might find them?

I can get grassfed fat at a local health food store. But what would I be putting it on if I’m not eating veggies? Meat? It seems unhealthy to me to be adding BEEF fat to say..FISH..just doesn’t seem natural.

While on the topic of fish, after doing a lot of reading about the microbiome’s importance and how bacteria affect us i have to say i don’t know if I’m willing to eat raw meats especially if they have been sitting out in open air at the grocery store. How does one justify and dive into this?

I eat such a low calorie diet because of my past with eating disorders-it has gotten me to where my body gains like crazy when I increase or get out of control. I understand there is more to a calorie but the fact is with where I am right now any more and i will gain.

I feel pretty good about the other factors-sunlight, walking, etc. I live in colorado and take walks and get in the sunlight often during the day. Allowing myself to get cold too and not using the heater near as much. But I know i need to work on eliminating EFTs at night-sooo difficult!!!

Also- I would love for someone to address the keto/insomnia issue as this is really going to decline my health quickly if i don’t address it.

Thank you so much for the help guys. So many questions, so much to learn. It can be a bit exhausting after 6 years of struggling!:/

### Linz — 2015-01-09T11:41:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leaky-gut-protocol-optimizing-ketosis.12560/post-152207>

Hayley, I think you need to concentrate on the circadian context. Ketosis goes with winter and cold, dark conditions. Cold promotes uncoupling so you burn free heat (brown fat) rather than storing fat or increasing inflammation. Do some cold water ct and see if you feel glowing heat generation in the night. Fat provides lots of electrons to keep the system running. With cold you need lots of fat. In summer lots of sunlight helps protect from the excess inflammation due to more carbs in the diet provided cell membrane potential (redox) is high enough. At the moment perhaps you should work on boosting your redox potential (leptin reset - daily timing issues).

There is a seasonal context to the fats too. Mid winter you want more omega 3, in autumn omega 6 can be higher (nuts etc) and saturated fats are fine in summer. You are right about not mixing fish with sat fats as they block the omega 3s from the fish. Use olive oil or ghee (if OK with your ai diet) or bacon fat with the fish. Coconut oil is really a summer fat but Dr K says it can be used all year round with ai issues - also cold and ketosis can be maintained all year with ai. Forget the calorie aspect and add lots of fats to your green vegetables and never skim broths.

Sorry I can't help with the eating disorder aspect except that I think understanding the cold/burning off heat rather than gaining weight link along with lots of DHA will help. Sleeping well requires cold and dark but it is difficult to tolerate cold if you can't generate free heat well

> Do as much seafood as you can! And yes, raw is better. I do canned only in emergency. You can make delicious meals from raw seafood...... I mostly add just ACV/lime juice and salt and if it is not a fatty seafood i add some EVOO or/and butter
> .

Inger,

Where would you recommend buying/ordering fresh or dried seaweed and raw fish from? I haven't stumbled across much in local stores except for dulse/kelp supplements.

Additionally, shouldn't I be skeptical of the safety of consuming raw seafood from a local grocery store even if it is wild caught? Any pointers on finding a good source?

Thanks for your help

David

If "Cavemen Callag" would not have been able to eat something, you should not either. Jack points out in his book that human brains evolved faster than human bodies, in a way, because our brains let us create environments that our bodies haven't had the time to evolve to be able to process properly yet.

I found that to be incredibly sensible, personally!

# Thread: NTIA readies 100MHz in 3.5GHz spectrum for release
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ntia-readies-100mhz-in-3-5ghz-spectrum-for-release.12567/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Penny — 2015-01-10T05:18:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ntia-readies-100mhz-in-3-5ghz-spectrum-for-release.12567/post-152293>

The US government has cleared the way for Google to become your next cellphone network provider – possibly without costing the company a pretty penny.

As we [revealed yesterday](http://www.theregister.co.uk/2015/01/08/googles_plan_to_become_your_phone_company/), the advertising giant wants to use the largely open spectrum at 3.5GHz to provide high-speed mobile data connections at low cost to phones and gadgets in cities.

The main stumbling block, for Google, is getting Uncle Sam to open up the radio space in a way that would give the company exclusive access to specific frequencies without having to buy an expensive license; cellphone networks typically spend hundreds of millions of dollars on buying the rights to use the ether.

Another hurdle is that the electronics in the vast majority of today's mobes and fondleslabs can't use that 3.5GHz slot, but it is in one of the [approved bands for LTE](http://niviuk.free.fr/lte_band.php). Another problem is that 3.5GHz signals will be rather short-range, so it's only good for cities and towns where lots of small cells can be deployed.

In a [release](http://www.ntia.doc.gov/blog/2015/promoting-spectrum-sharing-wireless-broadband-era) titled "Promoting Spectrum Sharing" put out on Friday, the US Department of Commerce's National Telecommunications and Information Administration ([NTIA](http://www.ntia.doc.gov/about)) announced that it is "to collaborate with the Federal Communications Commission (FCC) to free up critical radio spectrum to fuel the breakneck growth of the wireless broadband market," noting that the issue is "more pressing than ever, with the wild popularity of smartphones and tablets driving unprecedented commercial demand for mobile bandwidth."

The NTIA then talks about the two bands – at 1695-1710MHz and 1755-1780MHz – that are being auctioned off for $44.7bn as of today.

But the bulk of the announcement is about the issue of "spectrum sharing" that is needed for more space to be allocated to non-government organizations. The 3.5GHz slot that Google is interested in, for example, is used by the Department of Defense.

"The future lies in sharing spectrum," says the NTIA, "across government agencies and commercial services, and across time, geography and other dimensions in the future."

On the key 3.5GHz band, the release notes: "We are collaborating with the FCC to explore the possibility of making 100MHz of spectrum available for shared small cell use in the 3.5GHz band currently used primarily for military radar systems. This could be an important pivot point toward a new sharing paradigm."

Which in plain English means: by the end of next year Google may offer you an unlimited data plan on your cellphone for much less than what you are currently paying – assuming Google can lay its hands on the frequencies it wants for free or a price it's willing to pay. It will have to reach into its deep pockets if someone else shows an interest; so far there's no mention of payment, but the FCC has said an auction will be held if more than one outfit wants exclusive rights to a frequency slot.

The announcement also notes that the government is looking at "the feasibility of increased sharing by unlicensed devices in the 5GHz band. And we are working with federal agencies to assess their spectrum usage in five bands accounting for 960MHz to prioritize bands for detailed sharing feasibility studies." ®


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# Thread: Top Ten Tensegrity Tips
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/top-ten-tensegrity-tips.12579/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 17
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nonchalant — 2015-01-11T20:09:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/top-ten-tensegrity-tips.12579/post-152419>

We all know that the best ways to improve tensegrity are sunlight, CT, or exercise like yoga. But what about those times when you are unable to hang from a mountain by your fingertips? How can you get that extra burst of energy to help you sleep or stay awake, to stop coughing or clear a stuffy nose, or maybe even to figure out what Jack is saying?

My current favorite technique is to gently rake the skin of the arms and legs with the back of the fingernails. The skin is stimulated and it tingles, without scratching the skin. Works like a charm. Feels like goosebumps. The backs of the toenails work well for the arch of the other foot while you are in bed. I've noticed my flabby parts don't respond ideally, but if you are persistent, they will come around.

### Josh — 2015-01-11T21:02:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/top-ten-tensegrity-tips.12579/post-152422>

For me the technique depends upon the time of day, but sitting meditation is the favorite with the most consistent results. One can actually sit and visualize almost any other technique imaginable, including complex yoga and qi gong movements and get the same effects. Just letting go and watching works the best for me at this point.

### Marie — 2015-01-11T23:17:40+0200

He noticed me watching him, and he told me it helped him stay awake. A decade or so later I tried it, and discovered it works quite well. But I used a brush. Trying to comb my mop would probably get me in a wreck...

I wonder if that is part of the tradition of 100 brush strokes before bed? Yeah, I tried that... once.

When we neared the city, I noticed I couldn't get much reaction from my skin. Not many tingly feelings. And I started getting thirsty. When we finally started back home that evening, I was having problems with keeping comfortable in the cool temps. That night, back home, I started a fire, but even after the rest of my body warmed up, my feet were very uncomfortable. I figured I was dehydrated and would have to wait days to recover.

The next morning I had to help DH warm his icy feet, which hasn't been a problem for a year or so. I followed my usual morning routine, and around noon tried my tensegrity brush again. Wow was it zingy!! I rubbed my arms and legs, and my skin felt like I had just stepped into a CT bath. I was so relieved! # Thread: Bad news: caught a cold.  Good news: biohack!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bad-news-caught-a-cold-good-news-biohack.12580/>
**Subforum:** Biohacking 101
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nonchalant — 2015-01-11T21:04:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bad-news-caught-a-cold-good-news-biohack.12580/post-152423>

Wow, first cold in years! I blame the move. Once we were finally moved, the sun stayed behind clouds for 2 days. Throat, nose, ears, and lungs all involved. Once the sun re-appeared, I felt pretty good during the day. Then an hour or two before sunset, I would start feeling the cold temps. I would huddle around the fire, and when I went to the cold bed, I Did Not Shiver for my usual 5-10 minutes, though I still warmed up almost as well as usual. I never realized how much energy shivering takes.

A few hours before sunrise, all hell broke loose, thanks very much, Cortisol! But once the sun rose, the constant throat-clearing and coughing would stop and I'd start feeling good. After some sun bathing, I could garden, chop wood, even CT. But then in the afternoon I'd feel chilled, and the cycle repeated.

So how do I get more energy at night? Sun is gone, carbs and blue light are unacceptable. I'd been drinking water constantly already. Magnetico as always. IR light is great for chasing chills, but that's about it. I was using my tensegrity technique of firmly stroking my skin with the fingers to help me sleep, but my sleep was still disturbed often. I noticed that tensegrity seemed to help with the throat-clearing though. I was encouraged, and tried something a little stronger, using the backs of the fingernails. Woah! Very effective! See this post: [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/top-ten-tensegrity-tips.12579/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/top-ten-tensegrity-tips.12579/)

Twice I was able to stop coughing fits just with my fingernails. Amazing. So I am grateful for whatever bit me. Oh, I must confess, I ate a few slices of red bell pepper DH offered me. That gave me a scratchy throat right away, since they are out of season. Whatever bug was around saw the opportunity and grabbed it pronto. The scratchy throat never went away, and progressed into the illness.

I'm good now, but for the last couple nights I still only shiver a tiny bit when getting into that cold bed. I really miss it, because shivering puts me to sleep very quickly.

### Inger — 2015-01-11T22:31:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bad-news-caught-a-cold-good-news-biohack.12580/post-152432>

You might have caught the bugs from Nicole..... lol
Maybe the bell peppers were not organic and your body wanted to get rid of the poison right away?

I will try your finger trick

### Jude — 2015-01-11T22:57:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bad-news-caught-a-cold-good-news-biohack.12580/post-152434>

Nothing like moving to bring on a little stress eh.. Glad you had the rx down pat!
What's this cold bed you mention?...Guess it's my almost permanently warm environment that can't envision beds that are really cold??? Good to have you back on board..missed you!

### nonchalant — 2015-01-12T00:17:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bad-news-caught-a-cold-good-news-biohack.12580/post-152447>

Thanks, Jude. I've missed everyone so much, but I thought about you all every day. In fact, I have seen the title of the new blog, and I wonder if that is where I got my new idea about the smoke ring vision effects I've seen lately. The coincidence of thinking something only to find it recently posted on this website happens often enough that it is no longer weird.

### Inger — 2015-01-12T09:14:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/supplements.12581/post-152482>

Well.... if you have health issues I would not drink wine in the night....... especially if you have no great sleep. It sure hurts my sleep if i do (mostly) If you want to have wine, drink only 1 (!) glass, and before 5 PM, and it should be organic.... and a high resveratrol one like Argentinian Malbec. Alcohol also dehydrates you so if you have dehydration issues i would not drink any.......

I think wine is fine if in ketosis, but it will disturb the fat burning i have read...... at least for that evening. so if you want to lose weight I would not recommend drinking any alcohol - sure people might react different so you can try your own N=1  I sure do not do too well with alcohol... weirdly enough i have zero desire for it lately too....... I bought be a bottle of wine and i just do not want it... tried to drink a glass but my whole body and mind says no... so weird! I think some alcohol with good friends is also a different thing that when you drink it alone... seems i can tolerate it in a different way then...... that is very weird and i have no clue how it is but it must have something to do with quantum magic...lol
I think it really depend on the context.......

If you want chocolate for health issues, I recommend to use raw chocolate..... I can feel a difference between raw and heated cacao. I think the health benefits are way better from raw. And do 90% + cause you do not want the sugar in it!  especially not if you are in winter time..... You can buy raw organic cacao and mix it with coconut oil etc. = make your own chocolate

Seaweed is best eaten fresh but if you can get raw, dried seaweed you can soak it and drink the water and puree the rest into a smoothie and drink it too  I do that often and I love it! I use different kind of seaweeds because they have different health benefits. In my food i use Dulce mostly, it is so good added to raw salmon, cut into thin pieces. I do not soak that, it get nicely soft from the juice in my salmon chevice
I really recommend eating seaweed. You need to read the Epi paleo rx, seaweed has a big place in it.... tells about the benefits of different kind of them.. it is huge!

Yeah... omegas from food is way better . If you think you have too much omega 6 just quit eating nuts and olive oil etc. for a while and go heavy on the seafood  then it will naturally balance out  I eat so much fatty seafood so i eat omega 6 on purpose...lol I use raw nuts... and sometimes EVOO too. But not every day at all. Just once in a while. In winter i eat less from the omega 6. In fall i ate quite a bit of nuts...lol But that is when they naturally appear here too, so i guess that is just fine

Interesting about your water... are you sure it is sulfur? When, then it is really great! I guess if your skin is great and hair and nails it could mean you get enough of it! If not you could still try the MSM as the excess will just be peed out.....

### Haley — 2015-01-12T23:35:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/supplements.12581/post-152597>

Inger, thank you for your wonderful replies I thoroughly enjoy and appreciate them.

My last thyroid panel was as follows:
FT4: 0.792, ref range 0.90 to 1.90
FT3: 1.920, ref range 2.0 to 4.40
TSH: 4.00, ref range 0.270 to 4.20
Reverse T3: 14, ref range 8 t0 25
Negative for antibodies

My question is can I improve these numbers without prescription medicine. My doc started me on Synthyroid, 25mg, and it made me really nauseous, so I stopped. I only took it for 2 days.

Other info: My ASI test is normal, but I have microcytic anemia and my gut is terrible. I just completed Cyrex Assays 3 and 3, as well as the Genova complete stool analysis. With this information I can start working on the gut.

I started the LR 3 months ago, CT 2 months ago, and I am eating a very strict Epi-Paleo diet.

My body temperature averages around 97.3, and I am cold all the time. (Makes CT very challenging.)

Thanks for all replies.

T4 is converted to T3 in the gut (20%) and in the liver (80%) showing that thyroid works to help protect our portal circulation from gut inflammation.
The increased secretion of TSH from the pituitary will affect the liver. It causes the liver to make hsCRP...here is the tie to leptin resistance.
If your gut is no good, the conversion of T4 to T3 will be impacted. hsCRP will be going up and creating leptin resistance at the hypothalamus, and probably locally at the liver...
a VAP lipid panel would no doubt show elevated sdLDL and decreased HDL, hsCRP would be valuable too...

### David H — 2015-01-12T07:24:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cfs-and-d3-puzzle.12583/post-152475>

I'm new here, so first I'll say hello! And sorry to start out on such a low note :-(

I've had CFS for almost 7 years and most of the associated symptoms. Hypersomnia 10+ hours, daytime fatigue, weakness, brain fog, neck pain, etc... Recently I decided to finally address my low D3 levels and on the 2nd day I had complete remission of all symptoms. This lasted for 3 days and then I relapsed. I now relapse and remit but there doesn't seem to be a pattern. The initial D3 was 8,000 IU, I've done lower and higher but it doesn't seem to help maintain remission. Also trying co-factors, especially Mg, to see if it helps but not so far.

This happened before with the liver support supplement Super Thisilyn (milk thistle, methionine, ALA, NAC, herbs). Similar in that I got great periods of remission but could not maintain it.

So, any ideas to what's going on? Glutathione, immune system, HPA, antimicrobial, mitochondria? I'll add some possibly relevant details below. Thank you in advance to anybody that can help solve this puzzle!

-   Clonazepam for 7 years, stopped in 2011
-   low D3 (16-24 ng/dL)
-   low TIBC and UIBC
-   almost all amino acids low on NutrEval except glutamic acid
-   TSH usually high but in range
-   low but in range free T3 and T4
-   very low neurotransmitters on urine test
-   constipation, high small intestinal methane gas
-   low reduced/high oxidized glutathione
-   MTHFR C677T heterozygous
-   elevated EBV, CMV, C. Pneumonia antibodies
-   Omega fatty acids good on NutrEval except very low DGLA
-   Perfect Health Diet 3.5 years

### Jack Kruse — 2015-01-12T17:15:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cfs-and-d3-puzzle.12583/post-152538>

Here is what I will say: Cholesterol and Vitamin D3 are nearly identical in chemical structure. Most people do not know this. The only difference is a single double bond in the second ring of the cholesterol backbone. This gives Vitamin D _**one less hydrogen atom**_ than the closed ring of cholesterol. After reading Tensegrity 6 that should make you think. One hydrogen is the only difference at the atomic scale. Here you will see the wisdom of Tensegrity 6 play its role. Hydrogen is the ultimate chameleon for cholesterol and Vitamin D3. What does it do? Everyone with this disease can't make pregnenolone from LDL cholesterol because they have pregnenolone steal syndrome..........all tied to the redox potential. When redox is low that one less hydrogen can't do what nature allows it to normally do.

(Noticed it is not diet related) When this happens and calcium is normal, both molecules remain sulfated. **DON’T FORGET THIS. THIS HYDROGEN INTERACTION WITH and WITHIN THE SUN IS HUGE.**

### Josh — 2015-01-12T17:59:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cfs-and-d3-puzzle.12583/post-152543>

This is really important and relevant from FB:

> -   Most alternative practitioners [Danny Scollan](https://www.facebook.com/danny.scollan) do not know the biophysics of how divalent cations and interact non native EMF. They blame on things that are not the proximal cause and this is part of the reason why some MD's remain skeptical of alternative claims. For example the non native radiation from handsets or blue tooth devices can affect the whole body because it conducts electricity and behaves like an antenna. It absorbs the radiation and generates eddy currents that can flow beyond the site of application. When they impinge on cell membranes, they weaken them by removing structurally important calcium and other divalent ions. This makes the membranes more likely to leak, which can give many biological effects. The human body makes a good antenna since blood vessels, which are low resistance pathways filled with a highly conductive salty fluid, connect virtually all of its parts. Even cell membranes, which have a high resistance to DC, allow radio‐frequencies through because of their high capacitance. This biophysics is not well known in most healthcare circles but there is a ton published about it and no one is making the connection. Instead we still see bad science on parasites, metals, and foods that cause a leaky gut. Allan Frey showed the basic science for the condition 50 years ago in military studies. It is known, just not well known by those who should make it their business to know better.
>     [30 mins](https://www.facebook.com/groups/872967099382852/permalink/940057089340519/?comment_id=940303929315835&offset=0&total_comments=10) · [Like](https://www.facebook.com/groups/872967099382852/940312239315004/?notif_t=group_activity#) · [1](https://www.facebook.com/browse/likes?id=940303929315835)
> -
>     [Jack Kruse](https://www.facebook.com/jack.kruse?fref=ufi)When you keep blaming foods for this condition you are showing a lack of scientific rigor. Sorry to be blunt but it must be said. Many hundreds of scientific studies suggest that the first effect of the eddy currents is to generate small alternating voltages across the cell membranes, which increase their permeability. This can have serious metabolic consequences as unwanted substances diffuse into and out of cells unhindered, and materials in different parts of the cell, that should be kept separate, become mixed. But how do these tiny alternating voltages increase membrane permeability?.
>     The answer lies in their ability to remove calcium ions from the membrane surface. We have known since the work of Suzanne Bawin and her co‐workers (Bawin et al. 1975) that electromagnetic radiation that is far too weak to cause significant heating can nevertheless remove radioactively labelled calcium ions from cell membranes. Later, Carl Blackman showed that this occurs only with weak radiation, and then only within one or more ‘amplitude windows’, above and below which there is little or no effect (Blackman et al. 1982; Blackman 1990). Then there is Becker Liboff (see Liboff et al. 1990) Wertheimer and Savitz work.
>     [30 mins](https://www.facebook.com/groups/872967099382852/permalink/940057089340519/?comment_id=940304165982478&offset=0&total_comments=10) · [Like](https://www.facebook.com/groups/872967099382852/940312239315004/?notif_t=group_activity#) · [1](https://www.facebook.com/browse/likes?id=940304165982478)
> -
>     [Jack Kruse](https://www.facebook.com/jack.kruse?fref=ufi)Calcium ions are positively charged calcium atoms. Free calcium ions normally occur in calcium salts but, like other positive ions, they can also bind to the negatively charged membranes of living cells. These membrane‐bound ions are in chemical equilibrium with the corresponding free ions in the surrounding medium, but there is a disproportionately large amount of calcium because it has two positive charges (i.e. it is divalent), which attracts it more strongly to the negative membrane. Most of the other readily available ions in living cells (e.g. potassium) have only one charge (i.e. they are monovalent). However, the extra charges on the divalent ions such as calcium and magnesium are literally their undoing. They let weak alternating electromagnetic fields remove them selectively from the membrane, which can have dire metabolic consequences.
>     [29 mins](https://www.facebook.com/groups/872967099382852/permalink/940057089340519/?comment_id=940304252649136&offset=0&total_comments=10) · [Like](https://www.facebook.com/groups/872967099382852/940312239315004/?notif_t=group_activity#)
> -
>     [Jack Kruse](https://www.facebook.com/jack.kruse?fref=ufi)A simple way to explain the selective removal of divalent ions is to imagine trying to harvest ripe apples by shaking the tree. If you don’t shake it hard enough, no apples fall off, but if you shake it too hard, they all fall off. However, if you get it just right, only the ripe ones fall off and are ‘selectively harvested’. .
>     We can apply the same logic to the positive ions bound to cell membranes. Alternating voltages try to drive these ions off and then back onto the membranes with each half cycle. If the voltage is too low, nothing happens. If it is too high, all the ions fly off, but return when the voltage reverses. However, if it is just the right, it will tend to remove only the more strongly charged ones, such as divalent calcium with its double charge. Since at least some of these divalent ions will probably be replaced at random by other ions when the field reverses, there will be a net removal of divalent cations. However this occurs only within a narrow range of field strength to give an amplitude window. .
>     There may be more than one window. Blackman discovered at least two for calcium removal from brain tissue. This may be because not all membranes are alike; for example, some may hold their calcium more firmly and need a stronger field to remove them. Also, the local availability of other ions to replace the calcium may affect the ease with which it is removed. Nevertheless, the general effect is that electromagnetic exposure within an amplitude window reduces the amount of calcium bound to the membrane.
>     [29 mins](https://www.facebook.com/groups/872967099382852/permalink/940057089340519/?comment_id=940304419315786&offset=0&total_comments=10) · [Like](https://www.facebook.com/groups/872967099382852/940312239315004/?notif_t=group_activity#)

[https://www.facebook.com/groups/872967099382852/940312239315004/?notif\_t=group\_activity](https://www.facebook.com/groups/872967099382852/940312239315004/?notif_t=group_activity)

# Thread: Bone broth makes me feel like rat shit
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth-makes-me-feel-like-rat-shit.12587/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 17
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Ben1983 — 2015-01-12T09:50:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth-makes-me-feel-like-rat-shit.12587/post-152490>

Does anyone have any ideas as to why bone broth makes me feel irritable and slightly agitated after I drink it? I use bones from grass fed and finished cattle, reverse osmosis water, lemon or apple cider vinegar and I cook on low in a slow cooker for 24 hours. I've also cooked it for 30 hours and 48 hours on other occasions, but it doesn't seem to make any difference to how I feel after having it.

### Inger — 2015-01-12T10:40:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth-makes-me-feel-like-rat-shit.12587/post-152494>

I have read somewhere about it... it was a reason for it.. but i cannot remind me of what it was! I just know i have had that too.... sometimes... long ago.

You could try a fish bone smoothie to get the benefits from a bone broth? I have used herring bones leftovers from filleting herring. I just run them in my mixer with water and it turns out very smooth. I think that is a even healthier way to get the minerals in

### Ben1983 — 2015-01-12T12:35:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth-makes-me-feel-like-rat-shit.12587/post-152503>

Thanks, Inger. I might try that....come to think of it, I do eat sardine bones regularly....but they are tinned sardines so not sure how much goodness left in the bones...

### kovita — 2015-01-12T12:42:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth-makes-me-feel-like-rat-shit.12587/post-152504>

Ben, what could come to my mind is glutamin and glycin, because bone broth is very rich source specially if you use joints to prepare it. Some people do get too excited through potential glutamin excitotoxicity. You can easily test whether this is the culprit by trying pure collagen instead of the bone broth and experiencing the same issues.

### kovita — 2015-01-12T12:43:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth-makes-me-feel-like-rat-shit.12587/post-152505>

other way to test might be to get GABA after you consume it and see whether the symptoms go away...

### yewwei.tan — 2015-01-12T12:48:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth-makes-me-feel-like-rat-shit.12587/post-152506>

Anything else in the broth? There could be excess carbs in there, but I doubt the does is enough to be an issue.

Personal experience was that bone broth could lead to lots of bloating for myself. I initially though it may be cysteine related, but then realised that collagen contains very little cysteine. The viscosity of the broth could also affect gastric emptying rate, and could affect the absorption of nutrients from other foods.

Then some people may be sensitive to the histamines, to which a natural anti-histamine may help (like Quercetin from onions).

Also agreed with @[kovita](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/9805/) that glutamine could be an issue, but that also likely signals an issue with a leaky blood-brain-barrier (which is the only way GABA supplementation would work).

In any case, it probably has to do with gut flora modifications and resultant downstream neurotransmitter effects. (still waiting for @[Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) to give the gut flora blog talking about light emittence of these critters and how we use that light)

Why specifically do you want to drink bone broth though? Given your current context, you may try to go without it for awhile.

If the goal is to heal a leaky gut, maybe try some "animal fibre" -- See [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.ph...ntial-through-hydride-ions.10343/#post-118768](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/do-raw-foods-improve-redox-potential-through-hydride-ions.10343/#post-118768) and [http://caloriesproper.com/animal-fibre/](http://caloriesproper.com/animal-fibre/)

I haven't drunk bone broth in ages though, and definitely not since I've made my move to Cairns (where all my gut issues disappeared).

### kovita — 2015-01-12T13:04:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth-makes-me-feel-like-rat-shit.12587/post-152507>

thanks for the links, Yew! The animal fiber is interesting. I found myself chewing whole shrimps and even biting crab shells and any tendons/cartilages I found in my plate lately. It is just like I cannot leave it there, my kids are passing me all the skin from the fish and than have a great fun since this is the first think I eat. I lobe bone broth and it does me really good. I have just seen the latest intestine health tests results and must say my gut microflora is in better shape than ever before now, but cannot discount the pregnancy has something to do with....

### nicld — 2015-01-12T16:23:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth-makes-me-feel-like-rat-shit.12587/post-152523>

I just listened to Ben Greenfield’s pod cast on Kombucha and the guest speaker he had on said that some people react to Komucha the same way you are reacting to the bone broth because the bad bacteria are putting up a fight once the good ones are trying to move in.

Just a thought…….

### Ben1983 — 2015-01-12T16:30:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth-makes-me-feel-like-rat-shit.12587/post-152525>

Thanks for the responses. I have used Dave Asprey's BulletProof collagen before and had zero issues with it as far as I recall (it was over a year ago that I last purchased it). I might buy some again and see how I go on it. Yew, I ate quite a lot of onions with a meal straight after I had the bone broth so I'm not sure if it is a histamine issue. The reason I decided to try drinking it again was because I keep seeing video clips come come up in my newsfeed on facebook of various folks raving about all the health benefits from it....and so it just made me want to give it another go. My gut isn't too bad at the moment.....eggs still give me slight discomfort but nothing like before.

# Thread: salivary cortisol test - been awake 4 hours forgot to do it - too late today?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/salivary-cortisol-test-been-awake-4-hours-forgot-to-do-it-too-late-today.12594/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-12T16:18:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/salivary-cortisol-test-been-awake-4-hours-forgot-to-do-it-too-late-today.12594/post-152521>

\*sigh\*

I have a salivary cortisol test here from my doctor. One swab only. They said do it whenever and put the date and time on it and drop it off and they'll test it for me.

I wanted to do it first thing in the morning since I thought that would be most accurate. But I forgot and have been up for like 4 hours and drinking coffee the whole time.

Should I just wait until tomorrow to do it even though the results won't be back before my appointment on Friday if I do, or should I do it now? Does it even matter?

Thanks!

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-12T16:37:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/salivary-cortisol-test-been-awake-4-hours-forgot-to-do-it-too-late-today.12594/post-152530>

Oh dear, this is disheartening. I was not given instructions as to specific time frame, though when I asked the lab tech said she'd try to do it in the morning rather than evening, personally.

But now searching online for the past fifteen minutes found this:

"[http://www.labrix.com/SalivaCollection](http://www.labrix.com/SalivaCollection) - **The timing of the first morning collection is critical; it must be collected thrity \[sic\] minutes after awakening. Begin timing upon awakening even if you remain in bed.** "

Alright well I'll leave this thread here in case anyone else ever has this issue, at least this will turn up in search results.

They also didn't tell me to do anything else but rinse my mouth with water before I do the test and don't swallow the water (she stressed this very strongly).

So, here are the full set of specifications from the link above which I should've been told but wasn't -

-   Wash hands and rinse mouth with cool water before collecting saliva.

-   Wash face before bedtime the night before collecting saliva, and do not apply lotions or creams that night or next morning (some may contain interfering substances). Use of routine cosmetics is permitted.

-   Do not brush, floss teeth or have dental work done 30 minutes prior to collecting saliva. Slight bleeding from these may alter test results.

-   The timing of the first morning collection is critical; it must be collected thrity minutes after awakening. Begin timing upon awakening even if you remain in bed.

-   Avoid food one hour prior to saliva collection times. Food particles will contaminate the sample.

# Thread: January 2015 Webinar
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/january-2015-webinar.12595/>
**Subforum:** Redox Rx
**Posts:** 55
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2015-01-12T21:29:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/january-2015-webinar.12595/post-152581>

How much do you know about GPS? Did you know that your own GPS system is what controls your circadian cycles and every single biochemical reaction in your body. This includes how the organ clocks and how they yoke to the SCN..................

I have a sense all the complex blogs and going to make this webinar popular. You will now know why circadian signals trump food.

It is a counterintuitive position I hold dear.

So in your life you think you can also find solitude when you want it. But you're also mistaken.........no matter where you are in this world or how remote you may be you are connected to wave forms from the planet atmosphere and from space. Those connections or lack of connection via your choice are what create the life you get and live. This also will be hard for people to swallow.

Hard core scientist don't believe in magic. I do believe in an everyday sort of natural magic that is based in the most fundamental principles of QED -- the inexplicable connectedness we sometimes experience with places, people, works of art and the like; the eerie appropriateness of moments of synchronicity; the whispered voice, the hidden presence, when we think we're alone, yet we never are...............

Many believe we can dream by night, and escape your life, and we can dream by day is to make life happen...........both are false. We are always connected to an energy source whether we sense it or not.

I believe it is the natural unknown reality that draws us to science and biology. We just are yet to capture her whisper of how things really work.

Many stories we unfold in webinars magnify nature's recipes.

Everything made by human hands looks terrible under this type of magnification--crude, rough, and asymmetrical. But in nature every bit of life is lovely and flowing. And the more magnification we use, the more details are brought out, perfectly formed.

Modern science is so confident in what they think they know it breeds overconfidence in human thought and blurs out the risk inherit in it. When you come here to see my thoughts, I show you how little we really know, and in the light, our inferiority magnifies our species modern risk.

### Josh — 2015-01-14T03:08:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/january-2015-webinar.12595/post-152702>

For me, elevation has had a profound effect on my cognitive function and mood. I became the most depressed and unstable when living at 6650 feet in the Rockies=suicide belt. At sea level I am never quite right. At 9000 plus I fly, but have never spent more than a couple weeks at that altitude without a break. At my current altitude of 4300 feet with 340 days of sun, I am perhaps the most stable and balanced that I have ever been. There are many other factors, but I notice the difference when I descend to Phoenix at 2500  or ascend to the Mogollon Rim and Colorado Plateau at 7000+. I will have to scale this data into the current webinar and see what I come up with. I seem to enjoy bright light at altitude more than at sea level.

### Josh — 2015-01-14T19:57:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/january-2015-webinar.12595/post-152749>

After a second listen, the implications for how our body receives light and temperature and its interpretation seems more finely nuanced than ever. I always notice how differrent I feel when I buzz my hair down to 10mm. I feel a noticeable difference when I go to 4mm or even shaved. I always have felt as if I had a blanket on my head when I allow my hair to grow longer. I notice similar affects with different body coverings including different types of materials, thicknesses, areas and covered and more. Above all is the difference between blocking the eyes with a dark full block blindfold and not. I need to do this a couple of times most days to feel in balance. I imagine that this blocks the input to the SCN and changes the PVN output and more. I often open my eyes into blackness inside of the blindfold I use and this feels shall we say "relaxing"...The webinar suggest that we can really fine tune the messages to our system all the way down the scales if we understand how it works and at what gradient it responds. Quite thrilling really.... It begins to prove much of what is claimed, but inconsistently delivered by traditional medical systems such as Oriental Medicine.....

### Josh — 2015-01-15T05:11:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/january-2015-webinar.12595/post-152802>

I was trying to explain the webinar to my partner. When I explained that a nanosecond=1 billionth of a second which means there are a billion of them in a second and that an error of this size in the atomic clock of a GPS satellite could cause a 1 foot error on Earth, she started to see how even the few feet between the organs and SCN or the altitude that the body lives at could have very real effects in a body where distances are much smaller than between the Earth and an orbiting satellite. It seems to me that we are working with micrometer and nanometer distances between cells and organelles such as mitochondria reckoned against the feet/up to a meter distances between the SCN and the organs. The ratios seem similar to the surface of the Earth and the orbiting GPS satellites.

Let us say 400 miles above the Earth=2,112,000 feet

1 nanosecond error at sea level = approximately 1 foot/30cm error

ratio=1/2,112,000

Let us say 1 meter from adrenal gland to SCN=1,000,000 micrometers

Average diameter of a mitochondria=0.75 micrometer

ratio=1/1,333,333

This is rough to demonstrate that the effects may well be of similar magnitude

### Da-mo — 2015-01-15T05:15:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/january-2015-webinar.12595/post-152803>

Following the introduction of altitude affected time variation . . . lots of questions . . .

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gravitational\_time\_dilation](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gravitational_time_dilation)

Add to this that the Earth is 3 million miles closer (3%) to the sun in January than in July due to elliptical orbit.

[http://www.cso.caltech.edu/outreach/log/NIGHT\_DAY/elliptical.htm](http://www.cso.caltech.edu/outreach/log/NIGHT_DAY/elliptical.htm)

> The time for the Earth to turn this small angle is about four minutes. This little difference would cause no concern if it were always the same, but it is not! Recalling that the Earth moves in an elliptical path (much exaggerated in Fig. 1) around the Sun, rather than a circular path, it turns out that the Earth is nearer to the Sun in January than in July. The difference is about three million miles (out of an average distance of ninety-three million miles).

[http://www.cso.caltech.edu/outreach/log/NIGHT\_DAY/elliptical.htm](http://www.cso.caltech.edu/outreach/log/NIGHT_DAY/elliptical.htm)

Remember that while this difference may seem small, the intensity of all of the fields suspected of causing time dilation follow an inverse square law with distance.

>
> The divergence of a [vector field](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vector_field) which is the resultant of radial inverse-square law fields with respect to one or more sources is everywhere proportional to the strength of the local sources, and hence zero outside sources. [Newton's law of universal gravitation](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton%27s_law_of_universal_gravitation) follows an inverse-square law, as do the effects of[electric](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electricity), [magnetic](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Magnetism), [light](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Light), [sound](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sound), and [radiation](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Radiation) phenomena.

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inverse-square\_law](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Inverse-square_law)

Not only does our altitude with respect to the sun change over the year (with possible gravitational time dilation) but our speed through space changes as well.

> The speed of the Earth in its orbit increases as it gets nearer to the Sun. Since the Earth is closest to the Sun in January and furthest in July, it follows that the Earth is moving more rapidly in its orbit in January than in July!

[http://www.cso.caltech.edu/outreach/log/NIGHT\_DAY/elliptical.htm](http://www.cso.caltech.edu/outreach/log/NIGHT_DAY/elliptical.htm)

So what are the effects on quantum time of a change in velocity when moving through:

-   gravitational fields?
-   magnetic fields?
-   relative to incoming light?

As the Earth moves closer to the sun, even though its line of travel is tangential to incident light:

-   would this add/subtract to the angle of incidence of electromagnetic radiation - must the same way as apparent wind direction is affected by the speed of a sailboat?
-   would this also cause a slight redshift or blueshift depending whether out altitude was increasing or decreasing?
-   would the angular momentum of the spin of the planet be additive to decreasing solar altitude in the morning and subtractive from decreasing solar altitude in the afternoon . . . and conversely be subtractive to increasing solar altitude in the morning and additive to increasing solar altitude in the afternoon?
-   if all of the above could affect redshift and blueshift - what would be the effects on quantum timing?

Finally . . . the elliptical orbit (change in solar altitude and observed size of the sun), axial tilt, acceleration and deceleration of orbital speed imply that solar radiation on Earth is more intense and longer in duration during summer (and less intense/shorter duration during winter) in the Southern hemisphere than the Northern hemisphere.

-   Could we therefore also extrapolate that the Southern hemisphere experiences more seasonal time dilation compared with the Northern?
-   Could we then take advantage of these time dilation effects by seasonally changing location between hemispheres?
-   Do seasonally migratory animals take advantage of these time dilation effects to re-calibrate their quantum clock speeds and increase longevity?

Dr. K has often said that zip code is very important . . . .

-   does the ideal location change with the seasons due to quantum time variation?
-   has evolution selected for migratory patterns where this aspect is a factor?

### Jack Kruse — 2015-01-15T05:25:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/january-2015-webinar.12595/post-152807>

Light has a universal speed limit at 186,000 miles an hour. Light travels 30 centimeters in one nanosecond. The only way to increase its energy is to increase its frequency. If timing is off distant signals will also be awry. This very same feature is why GPS clocks orbiting the Earth must run faster than clocks on the ground to navigate properly. They are 38 microseconds faster. In 38 microseconds at the speed of light, if this clock difference did not exist, GPS devices would be off by over ten kilometers a day. When this happens in our brains’ mitochondria the inaccurate timing results in things like neuro-degeneration, T2D, and autoimmunity. Add to this that the Earth is 3 million miles closer (3%) to the sun in January than in July due to elliptical orbit.

So what are the effects on quantum time of a change in velocity when moving through:

gravitational fields?

magnetic fields?

relative to incoming light?

These are the things mitochondria and water pay deep attention to so you don’t have too.

It is also why ancestral health, paleo, and medicine are so off track; because it is not food that matters most but timing, especially circadian timing and meal timing that do..........inconvenient truth for sure...........but the truth is not diminished by the number of people who believe it.

### Josh — 2015-01-15T06:01:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/january-2015-webinar.12595/post-152812>

It struck me as I was falling asleep last night that while we had covered that pregnenolone and all hormones start with production on the inner mitochondrial membrane, I really had no idea how this goes. Looking at it, I notice the P450SCC enzyme that must rely on proton tunneling that is dependent on distances, mass equivalence and such. Regulation of these would probably require a good clock as even small time irregularities would change the distances, proton tunneling and synthesis rates. I realize that sensing of seasonal changes and the adjustment of hormone levels for breeding at optimal time and in optimal conditions might indeed be more or less dependent upon this as well. Extrapolate this out to girls menstruating at 10 and under and so on...... Perhaps their blue light saturated bodies have an SCN that has essentially told them that they are already 15 due to changes in clock speed....

### Josh — 2015-01-15T06:17:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/january-2015-webinar.12595/post-152813>

I realize that we have focused on the electron transport chain as if it is the only thing happening in a mitochondria. I am trying to fit it together with Pregnenolone synthesis to create a functioning image to play with. So far I have not found the right one. I am discovering that many more things happen in a mitochondria besides energy production through the electron transport chain such as: GABA synthesis, heme synthesis and more. I found an article on diurnal function in mitochondria suggesting that things are timed shall we say...

[http://www.mcponline.org/content/9/10/2125.full.pdf+html](http://www.mcponline.org/content/9/10/2125.full.pdf+html)

Fig. 5.
Metabolism scheme illustrating enzymes in mitochondrial primary metabolism with altered abundance or activity during diurnal cycle. A dark-enhanced phase (1–8 h; _red_), a midnight to early morning phase (10–14 h;_orange_), a day-enhanced phase (14–20 h; _green_), and a late-day phase (18–24 h; _purple_) are shown. One enzyme changed in dark-to-light and light-to-dark transitions (_blue_). _BCAT_, branched-chain amino acid aminotransferase; _E-CoAH_, enoyl-CoA hydratase; _FUM_, fumarase; _GABA-T_, GABA transaminase; _S-CoA-L_, succinyl-CoA ligase; _IVD_, isovaleryl-CoA dehydrogenase; _SSA_, succinic semialdehyde; _ETF_, electron transfer flavoprotein; _MDH_, malate dehydrogenase; _SDH_, succinate dehydrogenase; _2-OGDH_, 2-oxoglutarate dehydrogenase.

You would regulary see some very crazy things providing you sit with the results over "this synthetic map". Why would anyone had fantastic DHEA and the same time shit pregnenolone. Or fantastic progesterone and the same time shit pregnenolone... I think this may be when the lifestyle/environment/clock enter the game. ### Josh — 2015-01-15T17:53:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/january-2015-webinar.12595/post-152880>

I also noticed that sulfated pregnenolone is a big deal in the brain. I honestly had not even noticed that there was a sulfated form. Contemplating the importance of sulfur combined with hormones in light of all the new things we have learned about shall we say the "kingdom of sulfur"......

Need to look through this, part of a pretty good sex hormone review from someone who still knows precious little about such things free on our beloved internet:

[http://www.intechopen.com/books/ste...e/the-biological-roles-of-steroid-sulfonation](http://www.intechopen.com/books/steroids-from-physiology-to-clinical-medicine/the-biological-roles-of-steroid-sulfonation)

**FIGURE 1.**
Sulfate levels need to be maintained for sulfonation reactions to function effectively. _(A)_ In the kidneys, filtered sulfate is reabsorbed through epithelial cells in the proximal tubule via NaS1 on the apical membrane and then by SAT1 on the basolateral membrane. _(B)_ Intracellular sulfate is obtained from extracellular sources via sulfate transporters, and is derived from the metabolism of methionine and cysteine. Sulfate and ATP are converted to the universal sulfonate donor, PAPS. Both (_1_) sulfonation and (_2_) de-sulfonation reactions are active within intracellular metabolism. Sulfonated molecules are transported across the plasma membrane of cells via ATP binding cassette (ABC) proteins, sodium-dependent organic anion transporter (SOAT) and organic anion transporter polypeptides (OATPs), where they provide a circulating reservoir for cellular uptake and intracellular de-sulfonation. R-O-SO3 represents sulfonated substrates, including steroids.

**FIGURE 3.**
Steroid sulfonation and de-conjugation pathways play an important role in steroid metabolism, as well as regulating steroid half-life and activity. (_A_) Steroid sulfatase (STS) converts cholesterol sulfate to cholesterol, which is then transported into mitochondria for conversion to pregnenolone, and downstream adrenal products including DHEA and DHEA sulfate. (_B_) DHEA sulfate serves as the precursor molecule for synthesis of the non-adrenal steroid hormones, including testosterone and estrogens. In most cases, sulfonation decreases the biological activity of steroids by preventing binding to steroid receptors.

> Pregnenolone and its sulfate, like [DHEA](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dehydroepiandrosterone) and its [sulfate](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/DHEA_sulfate) and progesterone, belong to the group of [neurosteroids](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neuroactive_steroid) that are found in high concentrations in certain areas of the brain, and are synthesized there. Neurosteroids affect synaptic functioning, are [neuroprotective](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Neuroprotective), and enhance [myelinization](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Myelin). Pregnenolone and its sulfate ester are under investigation for their potential to improve cognitive and [memory](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Memory) functioning.[\[3\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pregnenolone#cite_note-pmid11744095-3) Pregnenolone is also being considered as a potential treatment for[schizophrenia](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Schizophrenia).[\[1\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pregnenolone#cite_note-pmid21756978-1)
>
> Interestingly, unlike pregnenolone, [pregnenolone sulfate](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pregnenolone_sulfate) is a [negative allosteric modulator](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Negative_allosteric_modulator) of the GABAA receptor[\[4\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pregnenolone#cite_note-pmid3138576-4) as well as a positive allosteric modulator of the[NMDA receptor](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NMDA_receptor).[\[5\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pregnenolone#cite_note-pmid1654510-5)[\[6\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pregnenolone#cite_note-pmid1387199-6) In addition, it has been shown to activate the [transient receptor potential M3](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/TRPM3) (TRPM3) [ion channel](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ion_channel) in [hepatocytes](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hepatocyte) and [pancreatic islets](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Islets_of_Langerhans) causing[calcium](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Calcium) entry and subsequent [insulin](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Insulin) release.[\[7\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pregnenolone#cite_note-pmid18978782-7)

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pregnenolone](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pregnenolone)

As I recall, sulfur has a lot to do with our ability to handle light, especially higher energy/frequency forms such as the sun...

### Josh — 2015-01-15T20:31:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/january-2015-webinar.12595/post-152897>

Interesting article which I have only skimmed, seems we have reviewed the implications of "mitofilin depleted mitochondria" before....

> The inner mitochondrial membrane is where the action of life happens. It is covered by some unusual proteins. [Mitofilin proteins](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/23109542) are crucial organizers of mitochondrial architecture. They are located in the inner mitochondrial membrane and interact with several protein complexes of the outer membrane, thereby generating contact sites between the two membrane systems of mitochondria. Here you can see how proteins undergo elastic changes as electrons are added and subtracted to its structure. When electrons are abundant, the mitofilin works to maintain ETC. When it is not, the result is autophagy or apoptosis. Within the inner membrane, mitofilins are part of hetero-oligomeric protein complexes that have been termed as the mitochondrial inner membrane organizing system (MINOS). MINOS integrity is required for the maintenance of the characteristic morphology of the inner mitochondrial membrane where ETC occurs. The inner boundary region is closely apposed to the outer membrane and cristae membranes, which form large tubular invaginations that protrude into the mitochondrial matrix and harbor the enzyme complexes of the oxidative phosphorylation machinery. MINOS deficiency comes along with a loss of crista junction structures and the detachment of cristae from the inner boundary membrane. MINOS has been conserved in evolution from unicellular eukaryotes to humans. Alterations of MINOS subunits are associated with multiple pathological conditions.

[http://jackkruse.com/organizational-structural-failure-6-mitochondrial-rx/](http://jackkruse.com/organizational-structural-failure-6-mitochondrial-rx/)

### Josh — 2015-01-15T20:52:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/january-2015-webinar.12595/post-152901>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=152808)
>
> Yes and it is magnified in water.

Do we have vestiges of the dual system that seals and whales have for hearing in air and in water?

> Conceptually, hearing is a relatively simple chain of events: sound energy is converted by neuro-mechanical
> transducers (middle and inner ear) into electrical signals (neural impulses) that provide a central processor (brain)
> with a window on the world. The view through that window differs dramatically for every species and, in part,
> reflects the selection pressures that shaped its ear. Structurally", the picture is more complex. Mammalian ears are
> elegant structures, packing over 75,000 mechanical and electrochemical components into an average area of I cm3.
> Structural variations in the components are essential elements in species differences in hearing abilities; therefore,
> comparing ears from species with different hearing ranges and habitats can provide useful insights into both specific
> and basic hearing mechanisms.
> Marine mammals, and whales in particular, are good candidates for such analyses. "Marine mammal" is a broad
> categorization for over 150 species from four orders. Extant groups of marine mammals include the Pinnipedia,
> which includes the true seals (phocids), sea lions (otariids), and walrus (odobenids); marine Fissipedia (sea otters);
> Sirenia (manatees and dugongs); and Cetacea, which comprise the baleen or "moustached" (mysticete) whales and
> echo locating, toothed ( odontocete) whales, porpoises, and dolphins. The degree of aquatic adaptation varies for each
> group. Many pinnipeds are amphibious, while all cetaceans and sirenians are aquatic obligates, unable to move,
> reproduce, or feed on land.
> All marine mammals, including whales, evolved from land-based animals with air-adapted ears (1). Over the next
> 50 million years, as their body shapes evolved to withstand enormous pressures, preserve heat, and move efficiently
> in the comparatively dense, cold environment of the seas, their ears evolved in tandem to effectively processes
> underwater sound. Water, with a density nearly 800-fold that of air (r=1.03 glee vs. 0. 0013 glee), has a sonic
> velocity that is on average 4.5 times faster than in air (c=l 530m/sec vs. c=340rn/sec). To examine the sensory
> implications of these values, consider a hypothetical mammal, that hears equally well in water and in air. An
> animal with an intensity based ear would require the same acoustic power/unit area in water as in air to have an
> equal sound percept, or (lair= !water):
> lair= Pai/1(0.442 g-rn/sec-cc) = Pwate/1(1575.g-rn/sec-cc) =!water (1)
> which implies the sound pressure in water must be 59.7 times that required in air to produce the same intensity and
> therefore the same sensation in this hypothetical perfectly matched amphibian ear. The units of intensity are, of
> course, watts/m 2, but to put this into the context of conventional hearing measures, recall that intensity is related to
> the mean square pressure of the sound wave over time. Most hearing studies report hearing thresholds in effective
> sound pressure level (SPL), expressed in decibels (dB), which in turn are complicated by arbitrary reference
> pressures. For air-borne sound measures, the reference is generally dB SPL or dB re 20 JlPa rms; for underwater
> sound measures, the reference pressure is dB re 1 JlPa. Thus, underwater sound pressure levels numerically are
> 61.5 dB greater than sound pressure levels in air for an equal intensity. In any case, whether viewed in terms of
> pressure or intensity, there are substantial differences for an equal percept
> Marine mammal ears have solved this problem, as well as all the attendant differences in other acoustic cues; e.g.
> shortened interaural arrival times. Equally important, underwater acuity, and the level of structural adaptation that
> underlie these abilities, do vary among marine mammals. Some, like the sea lion, still hear better in air, while
> others, like the bottlenosed dolphin, are fully aquatic, that is, they are exceptionally acute processors of waterborne
> sound but are probably incapable of hearing in air in any normal functional sense. Therefore, teasing out differences
> among these ears and comparing them to those of land mammal provides an opportunity to explore how structural
> elements in mammal ears relate to media dependent cues important for hearing.

And the post by @[Da-mo](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/9712/) is great too! -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/january-2015-webinar.12595/#post-152803](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/january-2015-webinar.12595/#post-152803)

\----

Anyway, let's attempt to put things together from both a high-level and low-level perspective.

At the high level: What is the goal of the cell in the first place?

Getting back to my Gedanken high-and-mighty creator-of-everything perspective: The goal of the cell is to read and react to environmental stressors using as little energy as possible; keep as many constituent particles and wave stationary, receiving information, and not needing to be advanced forward to their next state

Note: everything about wakefulness is a "stress" that requires a response

I also tend to like me Computer-Science oriented State Machine analogy, we can also call this "collapsing the waveform"​

\----

At the low-level, the goal is to focus as much information to the measurement device as possible. In this case, the measurement devices will be the SCN and the PVN.

I personally think that the PVN is placed alongside the Third Ventricle, which has a concentrating vortex during wakefulness, so that "memory" from the water in that ventricle is transmitted to the PVN.

I think this is a great way of measuring time effects using the natural Josephson Junction that occurs along the thin lining of axons between the third ventricle and the hypothalamus (provided that the axons are adequately myelinated) -- [http://www.riken.jp/en/research/rikenresearch/highlights/5682](http://www.riken.jp/en/research/rikenresearch/highlights/5682)

\----

Intuitively, I think that the greater a positive time dilation differences in timing information between the SCN+PVN and the body, the greater the error in measurement. Meaning that time passes faster for the SCN than the rest of the body => less window of opportunity for measuring things accurately.

Flipping that around is beneficial. Example: greater magnetic field and/or greater gravitational effect upon the SCN => greater time dilation effects => larger window of opportunity for accurate measurement

In that sense, I think more time for the SCN+PVN relative to the rest of the body is a good thing. I would also then think that greater overall time dilation effects in the early part of the year is beneficial thing to have while in a waking state.

(Note that if my logic behind a concentrating vortex also concentrating information is wrong, then the conclusions are flipped. ie: if a dissipative vortex helps the PVN get more info, then it's probably better to have greater time dilation effects in a sleep state, which is favoured during a circadian winter time)

### Josh — 2015-01-16T18:45:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/january-2015-webinar.12595/post-153028>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=152932)
>

So the dive reflex causes a shift to and or activation of whatever vestiges of this remain? This must cause time dilation as well via alteration of the PVN outputs.

> **6.** If you use just air to adapt to cold thermogenesis it will take a lot longer, but there is one thing I should mention to you. Try to slowly remove clothing as you proceed over time. As you remove clothing, there is a specific way you should pick the clothes to remove. You want to expose your face and head to cold as soon as possible. Remember in number 1, we begin cold water adaptation with our face? This is because all mammals have a reflex called a dive reflex that is built in because we all were formed in a fluid filled placenta. When we expose ourselves to cold on our face first, we stimulate slowing of our heart rate. This is soon followed by vasoconstriction of blood flow in our extremities. When we continue to dive deep (think about dive in the movie the Abyss) we force blood and water to pass through our organs and endothelium to fill our air filled cavities like our chest. This has been experimentally shown even in humans with deep water cold dives in adults. We actually drown in pulmonary fluids but we can still survive! As a physician, I see this problem clinically daily in our ICU’s in patients with ARDS. Sadly, we do not treat them as I think we should, given what we know about the mammalian dive reflex but that is another blog. Do not worry, I do not plan to use this adaptation in my reset in the near future! I’d love to try it but I hear it takes years to perfect from my research on it.

[http://jackkruse.com/the-evolution-of-the-leptin-rx/](http://jackkruse.com/the-evolution-of-the-leptin-rx/)

This must be what slows the heart rate?

Possibly relevant:

> These studies have shown that RVLM
> neurones are not alone in directly controlling sympathetic
> vasomotor outflow. Using the hypothalamic PVN as an
> example, I have tried to illustrate that the tonic activity
> of vasomotor nerves allows the PVN to initiate nonuniform
> patterns of sympathetic nerve activation, so that
> delivery of blood can be directed to organs where it is most
> needed. I show how this has led to the concept of
> ‘command neurones’ and that the new understanding
> has directed interest to changes in the brain as a cause
> of alterations in sympathetic activity in cardiovascular
> disorders such as hypertension and congestive heart
> failure.

[http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1113/expphysiol.2006.035378/pdf](http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1113/expphysiol.2006.035378/pdf)

**Figure 4. Major pontine and medullary regions involved in the cardiovascular and respiratory responses to peripheral chemoreflex activation**. Aortic and carotid bodies and supra-medullary pathways are not shown. Observe that peripheral chemoreflex activation recruits several brainstem nuclei in series and in parallel in order to produce a patterned and integrated cardiorespiratory response to hypoxia, which is characterized by (1) bradycardia, represented here as a decrease in instantaneous heart rate; (2) sympathoexcitation, demonstrated here as increases in the amplitude of thoracic sympathetic nerve activity; (3) tachypnea, show as an increase in the frequency of phrenic nerve bursts; and (4) forced expiration, here seen as an increase in the activity of the abdominal muscles during the expiratory phase of the respiratory cycle. NTS, nucleus tractus solitarii; NA, nucleus ambiguous; RTN/pFRG, retrotrapezoid nucleus and the parafacial respiratory group; BötC, Bötzinger Complex; preBötC, pre-Bötzinger Complex; rVRG, rostral ventral respiratory group; cVRG, caudal ventral respiratory group; RVLM, rostral-ventrolateral medulla; CVLM, caudal-ventrolateral medulla.

[http://journal.frontiersin.org/Journal/10.3389/fphys.2014.00302/full](http://journal.frontiersin.org/Journal/10.3389/fphys.2014.00302/full)

# Thread: urinalysis results: trace amounts of blood& ketones.. why?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/urinalysis-results-trace-amounts-of-blood-ketones-why.12608/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-14T14:10:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/urinalysis-results-trace-amounts-of-blood-ketones-why.12608/post-152729>

Hi everyone!
I just got a bunch of blood work back from my doctor this morning when I woke up - they were there in my online health portal but I haven't had a chance to go over them with him yet, and won't for some days because he's on vacation.

That being the case I thought I'd post at least some of it here to get some input.. and the thing that I am most curious about is the urinalysis results which stated "Abnormal Final Result" due to the presence of "Trace" amounts of ketones and blood in my urine.

When I hear "ketones in the urine" it makes me think of diabetes -- I am not diabetic but my mother was and my oldest brother is. I was tested for diabetes this summer and my glucose was at the top of the normal range. But normal.

When I hear "blood in the urine" it makes me think of a urinary tract infection -- I do not have one. I had one years ago and can't imagine that I have one now considering the pain I was in then, and the lack of it now. Plus, nitrites was negative.. not that you can always tell a UTI by that but still...

I also do not have and have never had issues with kidney stones. My liver and kidney bloodwork (Complete Metabolic Panel) was last done November 26 and it was all good. My urine Ph was normal at 6.5, with a standard range given as 5.0 to 8.0 (I understand Ph plays into formation of kidney stones but again I don't think that's the case here based on what I'm seeing, but I'm no doctor).

(In case it matters I am almost 42, not on any hormones at all yet, supplement with a woman's centrum daily multivitamin that I did not take prior to the test, and vitamin d3 that I did not take prior to the test. I was only on the d3 for two days before doing the bloodwork so can't imagine it would affect anything anyhow. I also take two fish oil pills per day but didn't take them that day either, and had only been on them for about a week prior to testing.)

I was wondering if anyone has any thoughts about this? Thanks!

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-14T14:23:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/urinalysis-results-trace-amounts-of-blood-ketones-why.12608/post-152730>

Here are the urinalysis results in their entirety:

**

LH MID-CYCLE PHASE: 27.3 - 96.1

POST-MENOPAUSAL: 0 - 31

### Hope2Learn — 2015-01-21T13:11:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-current-test-results-for-anyone-wanting-to-comment.12609/post-153526>

Serum DHEA testing came back this morning -
325 ug/mL

this puts me out of range on the HIGH end which is opposite of what I expected.

Problem is, I don't know if that's only because I took two 12.5 mg doses of DHEA in pill form about 3-5 days prior to the blood test. OR if it's truly high. (The standard range given by Quest Diagnostics, who my hospital/clinic uses and insurance will pay for, is "102 - 1185 NG/DL" without any specifics as to gender or age.)

How can I know? Anyone have an idea? Thanks as always!

\-----------------
[http://www.nlm.nih.gov/medlineplus/ency/article/003717.htm](http://www.nlm.nih.gov/medlineplus/ency/article/003717.htm) :

Typical normal ranges for females are:
Ages 18 - 19: 145 - 395 micrograms per deciliter (ug/dL)
Ages 20 - 29: 65 - 380 ug/dL <<<<<<< Mine was 325 when tested. I took two 12.5 doses of over the counter DHEA within 3-6 days prior to the blood test and got cystic acne immediately and stopped again. I just turned 42, BTW.
Ages 30 - 39: 45 - 270 ug/dL
Ages 40 - 49: 32 - 240 ug/dL
Ages 50 - 59: 26 - 200 ug/dL
Ages 60 - 69: 13 - 130 ug/dL
Ages 69 and older: 17 - 90 ug/dL
